concept.headline: Extension functions add new functions to existing classes without modifying them. this inside the extension refers to the receiver object.
concept.explanation: An extension function is defined as fun ReceiverType.functionName(params): ReturnType. Inside the body, this refers to the receiver instance. Extensions are resolved statically at compile time — they do not override members of the class and cannot access private fields. They are syntactic sugar: String.shout() compiles to a static utility function behind the scenes.
keyPoints: fun TypeName.funcName() — adds funcName to TypeName
this inside the extension = the receiver instance
Cannot access private members of the receiver
Resolved statically — member functions always win over extensions with the same signature

demo.intro: Watch two extension functions — one on String, one on Int — being called exactly like member functions.
demo.code:
fun String.shout(): String = this.uppercase() + "!"
fun Int.square(): Int = this * this

fun main() {
    val msg = "hello".shout()
    println(msg)
    println(5.square())
    println("world".shout())
}

explanation.what: "hello".shout() — calling the extension function on the string 'hello'
explanation.why: shout is defined as an extension on String — it appears as a method
explanation.intent: Show extension being called with dot notation — looks like a member function
explanation.misconception: shout is NOT actually added to the String class — the compiler rewrites this call to StringExtensionsKt.shout("hello") under the hood. The dot notation is syntactic sugar.

explanation.why: The extension body runs with this bound to the receiver 'hello'
explanation.intent: Show this referring to the receiver inside the extension
explanation.misconception: this is optional inside an extension — you can write uppercase() + "!" directly without this. It is only needed to disambiguate when there is a naming conflict.

explanation.intent: Show extension return value stored like any other function call
explanation.misconception: There is nothing special about how you store or use an extension function's return value — it behaves identically to a member function's return.
No special printing needed — msg is a regular String

explanation.what: 5.square() — extension on Int called on literal 5
explanation.why: Kotlin allows calling extensions on literals — 5 is a valid Int receiver
explanation.intent: Show extension on Int used fluently on a number
explanation.misconception: You can call extensions on any expression of the receiver type — including literals, variables, or function return values

explanation.intent: Show Int extension returning a computed value
explanation.misconception: Int is a Kotlin built-in type but you can still add extensions to it — you just can't modify its source code or add private fields

explanation.why: Same extension called on a different String receiver — this = 'world'
explanation.intent: Show same extension working on different receiver values
explanation.misconception: Extensions are not stored on the object — there is no overhead per instance. The function exists once in the compiled class file.

prediction.question:
What is the output?

fun String.repeat3(): String = this + this + this
println("ab".repeat3())
prediction.explanation: this = 'ab', so this + this + this = 'ab' + 'ab' + 'ab' = 'ababab'. String + concatenates without spaces.

explanation.afterDemo: Extension functions let you write fluent, readable APIs for any type — even ones you don't own. This is how Kotlin's standard library enriches Java's String, List, and other types with functions like reversed(), chunked(), joinToString() without modifying the original classes. Use them to make your domain types expressive.
keyTakeaways: fun Type.name() = ... adds name() to Type. this refers to the receiver.
Extensions are resolved at compile time — no runtime overhead, no virtual dispatch.
Cannot access private members, and members always win over extensions with the same signature.

practice.prompt: Write an extension function on List<Int> called sum(): Int that returns the sum of all elements. Print listOf(1,2,3,4,5).sum().
practice.hint: Iterate with for (n in this) and accumulate into a var total.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! 1+2+3+4+5 = 15. Note: Kotlin's standard library already has .sum() — this exercise showed you how it could be built.
retryHints: var total = 0; for (n in this) total += n; return total
this refers to the List<Int> receiver

summary.title: Extension Functions — Complete
takeaways: fun Type.name() adds a function to Type without modifying its source.
this inside the extension = the receiver object.
Extensions make APIs fluent and readable — the entire Kotlin stdlib enriches Java types this way.
